text: The surgeon operates through the right chest, creating a pneumothorax with the patient in a prone position. The collapsed lung is visible due to double lung ventilation. A plane is being created between the pericardium and the posterior aspect of the esophagus, with the pericardium visible anteriorly. Direct blood vessels from the aorta supplying the esophagus are coagulated and divided using a monopolar hook. The inferior pulmonary ligament is carefully released, detaching the lung attachment.

The surgeon proceeds caudally, exposing the diaphragm and creating a plane between the right crus and the esophagus. The lower part of the esophagus is fully mobilized. Moving cranially, a clear plane is visible between the esophageal vein and the esophagus, with the tracheal bifurcation and membranous part of the right bronchus also visible. A silk suture is used to ligate the azygos vein proximally to prevent overfilling, followed by clip application and division of the vein near the superior vena cava, marking the only structure divided in the thoracic part of esophageal mobilization.

The surgeon dissects the anterior aspect of the esophagus in the subcarinal region, carefully avoiding excessive diathermy near the membranous part of the trachea due to minimal space. The nodal tissues along the subcarinal region are cleared and placed in an endobag, revealing a completely mobilized esophagus. The patient is repositioned from prone to supine, and five ports are used for the abdominal part of the procedure. The gastrocolic omentum is opened, and a plane is created between the duodenum and the transverse mesocolon.

The surgeon carefully preserves the right gastroepiploic pedicle while dividing attachments between the anterior surface of the pancreas and the posterior surface of the stomach, extending up to the gastro-duodenal artery. The short gastric vessels are divided, maintaining proximity to the spleen to avoid undue traction on the stomach. The lesser omentum is opened, extending the incision cranially to the diaphragm, exposing the left gastric pedicle. The esophagus is detached from the diaphragm, and the inferior phrenic vessel is visible. The left gastric vein is mobilized, and nodal tissue along the left gastric pedicle is cleared.

The surgeon demonstrates the completely cleared nodes along the common hepatic artery. The left gastric artery is being divided, and further mobilization of the hiatus is underway. The retroesophageal window is visible, showing the fundus of the stomach. The nodes along the superior border of the pancreas and the course of the spleen are also completely cleared.

The surgeon clears nodal tissues along the left gastric pedicle, common hepatic artery, and splenic artery. The dissection reveals the anatomical course between the aorta and the esophagus, with the aorta's path clearly visible.

The surgeon makes a skin crease incision in the neck and divides the esophagus, attaching a Ryles tube to the distal segment. The esophagus is pulled into the abdomen through a small incision, and the specimen is removed. A gastric tube is created using a stapler, and the Ryles tube is attached to it. As the Ryles tube is pulled in the neck, the gastric tube is brought up to the neck. The procedure concludes with a pyloroplasty.
